body:
## Pull request overview

This PR introduces a composable `LabelTree` data structure to track labels from nested transactions, providing a hierarchical representation of transaction labels instead of the previous flat, outermost-only label.

**Changes:**
- Added a new `LabelTree<T>` class that represents an immutable tree of labels with Set-like operations (`has`, `values`, `forEach`, `size`)
- Extended `LocalChangeMetadata` and `RemoteChangeMetadata` interfaces with a `labels` field containing the composed label tree
- Modified transaction lifecycle in `TreeCheckout` and `SchematizingSimpleTreeView` to automatically compose label trees from nested transaction labels using `pushLabelFrame` and `popLabelFrame` methods

### Reviewed changes

Copilot reviewed 9 out of 9 changed files in this pull request and generated 4 comments.

<details>
<summary>Show a summary per file</summary>

| File | Description |
| ---- | ----------- |
| packages/dds/tree/src/util/labelTree.ts | New immutable tree data structure for composing labels with Set-like operations |
| packages/dds/tree/src/util/index.ts | Export the new LabelTree class |
| packages/dds/tree/src/test/util/labelTree.spec.ts | Comprehensive test suite for LabelTree functionality |
| packages/dds/tree/src/test/shared-tree/schematizingTreeView.spec.ts | Integration tests for label tree composition in nested transactions |
| packages/dds/tree/src/shared-tree/treeCheckout.ts | Implementation of label frame stack and label tree composition logic |
| packages/dds/tree/src/shared-tree/schematizingTreeView.ts | Integration of label frame push/pop with transaction lifecycle |
| packages/dds/tree/src/index.ts | Public API export of LabelTree |
| packages/dds/tree/src/core/rebase/types.ts | Extended metadata interfaces with labels field |
| packages/dds/tree/api-report/tree.alpha.api.md | API surface documentation for new LabelTree and labels metadata |
</details>

This whole "double linking" scheme is making the code significantly more complicated and hard to read than it would otherwise be.

1. Why do we need parent pointers? Because the "cursor" is pointing to the _bottom_ of the tree, so we need to be able to climb back up it to find the other nodes.
2. Why do we need to point to the bottom of the tree? Because we want to access the "current" label node in O(1). 
3. Why do we want to access the current label node in O(1)? Because... why not?
4. Why not? Because it makes the code way more complicated. Let's do something else.

`currentLabelTreeNode` - which we should rename to just `labelTreeNode` - can simply always point to the root node of the tree. Whenever you want to find the "current" node, walk down the right side of the tree. I.e. just do `node.children.at(-1).children.at(-1).children.at(-1)...` until you reach the bottom. That is the current node. A negligible performance impact (except in pathological cases), and in exchange we don't need parent pointers, which will dramatically simplify ValueNode and this push/pop code.

Same logic for removing the "make the tree as flat as possible" optimization. Everything is just way simpler if you have the LabelTree always be isomorphic to the actual transaction tree, whether there are labels or not. Even if there are no labels at all, but lots of transactions, still make the label tree have a node for every transaction. In the very unlikely event that this ever proves to be practically inefficient, we can optimize it at that time.
This is looking much cleaner!

There is no test coverage for transaction labels when a transaction is aborted (rollback). Consider adding a test case to verify that labels are properly discarded when a labeled transaction is rolled back, ensuring that aborted transactions don't pollute the label tree.

This code mutates a children array that will become part of a LabelTree, but LabelTree is documented as immutable and has a readonly children field. Mutating this array after passing it to LabelTree's constructor violates the immutability contract. Consider creating a new array when constructing each LabelTree node to ensure true immutability.

This code mutates a children array that will become part of a LabelTree, but LabelTree is documented as immutable and has a readonly children field. Mutating this array after passing it to LabelTree's constructor violates the immutability contract. Consider creating a new array when constructing each LabelTree node to ensure true immutability.

The documentation states "This is only defined when the outermost transaction has a label," but this is inaccurate. Based on the test case "inner labels are surfaced with undefined root when outer transaction has no label" and the implementation in popLabelFrame (lines 518-521 of treeCheckout.ts), labels can also be defined when the outermost transaction has no label but inner transactions do have labels (in which case a LabelTree with undefined root is created). The documentation should be updated to reflect this behavior.
```suggestion
	 * This is defined whenever at least one transaction in the nested stack has a label.
	 * If the outermost transaction has no label but inner transactions do, a {@link LabelTree}
	 * with an undefined root is created.
```

body:
Interestingly, there is nothing in this class that is actually specific to labels. It's really just a tree of values. So you have a choice:

1. Rename this to `ValueTree`, and adjust its documentation accordingly. It's now a generic tree of values (no mention of labels here) and can be used for other features besides labels, if ever desired.
2. Keep it as `LabelTree`, but double-down on the documentation. Talk about labels here, and how they are used in transactions, and cross-link to the other label APIs, etc. Basically, if you're going to make this a piece of code that can only be used by one feature (transaction labels), then benefit from that by strongly connecting it to that feature via docs and links.

body:
It's unfortunate that we have both `transactionLabels` _and_ `labelFrameStack`. Is it necessary?

If you follow [the suggestion here](https://github.com/microsoft/FluidFramework/pull/26405/changes#r2819221923), you should be able to accomplish this with an additional tweak. Have the customer-facing _interface_ be immutable, but have the implementation class (used in this file) be mutable. Then, you can just have a single field with a label tree and update it as the transaction open and close.

body: Cursor? I think I know what this is trying to say, but it's not clear what a cursor is at a glance.
What? How can the current node have children? Wouldn't the current node always be a leaf? I'm so confused lol

Having to update the parent of one node and the children of another - or else an invariant is broken - is a drawback of this "doubly-linked" scheme. Why doesn't the ValueTreeNode do this automatically? If you pass in an array of children, it will link both ways, but if you pass in a single parent, it will only link upwards.

This is an optimization to make the tree minimally sized but I don't think it's worth the code complexity. It only _happens_ rarely - when users have outer transactions with labels and also inner transactions without labels - and it only _matters_... never, probably. We would need hundreds or thousands of transactions inside of each other before any of this even begins to show up on a profiler.

Let's just do away with the parent pointers - see rationale in other comments. Data structures are so much simpler if they have clear, 1-way ownership. Parent nodes own the child nodes, no uppointers.

+/*!
+ * Copyright (c) Microsoft Corporation and contributors. All rights reserved.
+ * Licensed under the MIT License.
+ */
+
+/**
+ * A tree of values that supports recursive search and Set-like read operations.
+ *
+ * @remarks
+ * Each node in the tree has a value of type `T` and zero or more children.
+ *
+ * @sealed @alpha
+ */
+export interface ValueTree<T = unknown> {
+	/**
+	 * The value at this node.
+	 */

+

+	 */
+	has(value: T, areEqual?: (a: T, b: T) => boolean): boolean;
+

+	 * Iterates over all values in the tree in depth-first pre-order.

+}

body: Why do we need to special case aborts? If an abort happens, we should pop the frame. If a commit happens, we should apply the commit and then pop the frame. But I don't see why the frarme-popping itself should care, if that makes sense.
This should be `static`.

body: nit: I think best practice would be to have this return `ValueTreeNode | undefined`. The callers should determine whether to do the assert instead. For example, `pushLabelFrame` would assert before getting the `currentLabelNode`, but `closeLabelFrame` and `popLabelFrame` would tolerate the `undefined` and return early (as they currently do).

This is a curious optimization. Is there a strong rationale for it? I don't see any real harm in having the labelTree be present, even if there are no labels anywhere in it. `has` will still work (returning false always).

In fact... do you think we should _always_ provide a tree? It seems inconvenient from the user's perspective that they have to check whether `labels` is undefined. What if it were always defined, no matter what? Maybe `labels` should actually point to a "shell" object, which has the operations on it (like `has`, `values`, etc.). The shell would also have a `tree` property, which would contain what is currently `labelTreeNode`. The nodes themselves would then not necessarily need to have `has`, `values`, etc. - those could just be on the top-level shell.

Or, when firing the event, if `labelTreeNode` is undefined, you would instead just return an empty `ValueTree`. That's a little simpler, but also slightly inconsistent, because the shape of the value tree that the user gets no longer always exactly corresponds to the transaction structure. They can't tell the difference between a transaction that ran with no label and no transaction at all. Maybe we don't care.

What do you think of these different approaches?

Okay. What was your reasoning? What pros and cons did you consider when making your decision?

If the value can be potentially undefined, `has` is still easy: `labels?.has(...)`.
values is slightly worse, but not by much, since you can do: `labels?.values() ?? []`.

That said, it still seems maybe an unnecessary implementation detail being exposed. Suppose that `labels` was simply a `ReadonlySet<unknown> & { tree?: ValueTree }`. We could pretty easily offer that, and it'd be more familiar to the user. You _always_ get a Set, but it might be empty. This is more normalized than "you _might_ get a Set, but you have to check in case you didn't, but if you _do_, then it still only _might_ have something in it". It's better (normalized) to say either "you always get a set, but it might be empty" or "you might get set, but it will always have something in it" (and the former is generally preferable).

Or, here is yet another option:

* ValueTree doesn't have `has` or `forEach` - all it has is `values()`. This is _honest_, because `has()` is not O(1) - if a user is bummed that they have to iterate over the values rather than having instant lookup, well, we were iterating under the hood anyway.
* `labels` is an object of type `Iterable<unknown> & { tree?: ValueTree }`.

This leaves it up to the user what to do with the values - if they want to put them in a set, they can with `new Set(labels)`. If they're just looking for a single label, they can do `[...labels].includes("foo")` or if they have 30,000 labels and don't want to spread they can write a for loop lol.

These are all different design decisions with different tradeoffs. I'm not yet sure which I prefer, but I'd love to hear your thoughts!
All of this method's behavior is already present in `popLabelFrame`. I would delete this and just add an `aborted` flag to `popLabelFrame`.

1. The reader probably doesn't know what a "value tree" means
2. "auto-composed" isn't a particularly useful phrase. Auto, as opposed to manual? A reader won't know what composed means either, probably.
3. "nested transaction labels" - but also un-nested transaction labels. Nesting isn't required - this could be misleading.

The summary should be descriptive to a user, not necessarily descriptive of the mechanics of the thing. Like, what really is this thing? Why would I want it?

"A collection of labels for all transactions (nested or otherwise) that made up this change". Something like that.

Then in the remarks you can give the details and examples. Describe how the labels contribute to the tree structure, show an example of using `has`, etc.

body:
According to this logic you will break the first time that you find a last child that is closed, and will use its parent as the current node. When you find this closed-last-child, is it not the case that **it is always the most recently closed transaction**?

If it is indeed always the most recently closed transaction, then you do not actually need a WeakSet. You only need a single pointer to the "most recently closed transaction", which you update on pop.

Put another way, suppose you've marked 5 transactions in the tree as "closed" (by removing them from the set). But you could only possibly traverse to 1 of those 5, I think. That's the one to point to.
Do we need this class anymore? :) It doesn't do anything except copy the child array when constructing - probably not useful.

body:
Good question. A type conundrum (compile time) is usually not a good reason to make a runtime change. A mutable version would be a good solution.

But it might be even simpler than that. The `changed` event is the only way that a user is passed one of these things, right? We pass them our `checkout.labelTreeNode`. But then, we clear `checkout.labelTreeNode` (set it to undefined) before we ever edit it further, yes? So, we don't mutate a `ValueTree` after we pass it to the user. In which case, who cares if they mutate it. In fact, it might even be best practice to let them, because it's an output type - not an input type - that they take exclusive ownership of. Us telling them they can't modify it, when we don't even have a reference to it anymore, is perhaps silly and annoying.

So if you want, you can just make ValueTree mutable *shrug* both inside and out *shrug*. If we decide we need an immutable version later for some other theoretical feature, we can make the second type at that time.

body: I think this would be better as a generator that yields values. That is a more general and powerful function with a simpler implementation. And the usage for new sets is just `new Set(collectTreeValues(node))`

If the goal of doing this "pre-walk" is to avoid allocating the net Set below, then I think we could make it simpler.

1. Start with the set undefined `let set: Set<unknown> | undefined;`.
2. [ Use an iterator](https://github.com/microsoft/FluidFramework/pull/26405/changes#r2861404392) to walk the nodes `for const (node of collectTreeValues(labelTreeNode)) {`
3. If/when you find a node, do `(set ??= new Set()).add(node)`.
4. Return `set ?? emptyLabelSet`.

That way you only loop once, no need for `hasDefinedLabel`, but you still only allocate the new set if necessary.
nit: I'd prefer to see this kind of comment next to the actual algorithm, and the docs for this are more just like "used by {@link currentLabelNode}"

body: nit: Can you just declare the type of `set` as `TransactionLabels | undefined`, and then just do `set.tree = labelTreNode` here rather than `Object.assign`? Not sure if that works but it'd be slightly nicer.

If `ValueTree` is no mutable, do you want `TransactionLabels` to be mutable too? Should the `tree` property be mutable? Should the `ReadonlySet` be a `Set`? If so, you would need to create a new Set every time (you couldn't use your empty set singleton) but you could make it lazy, e.g.:

```ts
let set: Set<unknown> | undefined;
this.#events.emit("changed", {
  ...
  get labels(): {
    set ??= new Set();
    Object.assign(set, { tree: labelsTree });
    return set
  }
});
```

But I give this example to be academic - I think you should just always create a new set, even if its empty. From copilot:

**Prompt [How performant is constructing an empty set in JS? | Try in Copilot Chat](https://m365.cloud.microsoft/chat/entity1-d870f6cd-4aa5-4d42-9626-)**
How performant is constructing an empty set in JS?
**Response**
Short answer: **constructing an empty `Set` in JavaScript is extremely cheap**—so cheap that in almost all real code it’s effectively noise.

Here’s the more precise, performance‑oriented breakdown you’d expect as a JS/engine nerd.

***

## What actually happens when you do `new Set()`

For modern engines (V8, SpiderMonkey, JavaScriptCore):

*   `new Set()` allocates:
    *   A small JS object header
    *   An *empty* internal hash table (often lazily initialized or with a tiny initial capacity)
*   **No hashing, no buckets populated, no iteration**
*   Time complexity: **O(1)**
*   Allocation size: **very small** (tens of bytes)

V8 exposes this explicitly at the API level: `v8::Set::New(isolate)` is documented as “Creates a new empty Set” with no work beyond allocation. [\[v8.github.io\]](https://v8.github.io/api/head/classv8_1_1Set.html)

***

## How fast is it in practice?

While exact numbers depend on CPU, engine version, and GC state, the qualitative facts are consistent:

*   You can construct **millions of empty Sets per second**
*   It is comparable in cost to:
    *   `{}`
    *   `[]`
    *   `new Map()`

In other words, if `new Set()` shows up in a profile, **something else is wrong** (e.g. you’re doing it in a hot loop unnecessarily).

Benchmarks and engine discussions consistently focus on **operations on populated Sets** (add / has / delete), not construction cost, because construction is negligible relative to any meaningful work. [\[stackoverflow.com\]](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/39007637/javascript-set-vs-array-performance), [\[dev.to\]](https://dev.to/abdulghaffar349/optimizing-javascript-collections-comparing-sets-and-arrays-e07)

***

## Relative cost vs alternatives

Rough ordering (fastest → slowest), all O(1):

```js
{}            // object literal
[]            // array literal
new Set()
new Map()
```

But the gap between them is **tiny**. The difference is measured in **nanoseconds**, not microseconds.

Choosing between these should be based on:

*   semantics (uniqueness, iteration order, lookup complexity)
*   downstream operations

—not construction cost.

***

## When `new Set()` *can* matter

The only time it becomes relevant:

### ❌ Bad pattern (hot path)

```js
for (...) {
  const seen = new Set(); // repeated millions of times
  ...
}
```

If the loop is extremely hot and the Set escapes or grows, allocation + GC pressure can show up.

### ✅ Better pattern

```js
const seen = new Set();
for (...) {
  seen.clear(); // often cheaper than reallocating
}
```

Even then, **`clear()` vs reallocate** is workload-dependent and usually dominated by how many elements were in the Set.

***

## TL;DR

*   ✅ `new Set()` is **O(1)** and **very fast**
*   ✅ Cost is comparable to `{}` or `[]`
*   ✅ Safe to use freely in normal code
*   ❌ Only worth thinking about in extreme hot loops or allocation-heavy paths

If you’re worried about this, you’re already operating at a level where **algorithmic behavior and allocation patterns matter far more than constructor cost**.

If you want, I can:

*   show a microbenchmark pattern that *actually* reveals Set construction cost
*   compare `new Set()` vs reuse + `clear()` under different workloads

body:
## Pull request overview

This PR adds a `size()` method to the `Transactor` interface that returns the number of transactions currently in progress, including nested transactions. This is a simple enhancement that provides more granular information about transaction state compared to the existing `isInProgress()` boolean method.

**Changes:**
- Added `size()` method to `Transactor` interface with clear documentation explaining it returns 0 when no transaction is in progress, 1 for a single transaction, 2 for nested transactions, etc.
- Implemented the method in `TransactionStack` class to return the stack length with proper disposal checking
- Added comprehensive test coverage for the new functionality including normal operation and disposal scenarios

### Reviewed changes

Copilot reviewed 2 out of 2 changed files in this pull request and generated no comments.

| File | Description |
| ---- | ----------- |
| packages/dds/tree/src/shared-tree-core/transaction.ts | Added `size()` method to `Transactor` interface and implemented it in `TransactionStack` class to return the number of active transactions |
| packages/dds/tree/src/test/shared-tree-core/transaction.spec.ts | Added test case for `size()` method verifying it correctly reports transaction count through nested transactions, and added disposal test |

pr_title: Add ParentObject type used in new TreeAlpha.on method
body:
## Pull request overview

This PR introduces a new `ParentObject` type to represent non-TreeNode parents in the tree API, enabling unified handling of root, detached, and unhydrated nodes. The changes add `TreeAlpha.parent2()` which always returns a value (unlike the existing `parent()` which returns undefined for root nodes), along with corresponding updates to `child()`, `children()`, and a new `on()` method for event subscription that works with both TreeNode and ParentObject parents.

**Changes:**
- Added `ParentObject` sealed type with concrete implementations (RootParent, DetachedParent, UnhydratedParent) for representing different parent states
- Added `TreeAlpha.parent2()` method that returns `TreeNodeParent` (TreeNode | ParentObject), satisfying the invariant `child(parent2(node), key2(node)) === node`
- Added `TreeAlpha.on()` method for subscribing to events on TreeNodeParent, with status-change tracking for detached and unhydrated nodes
- Extended `child()` and `children()` to accept ParentObject, with key being `undefined` for non-TreeNode parents
- Added status change event tracking in TreeNodeKernel using a separate non-buffered event emitter

### Reviewed changes

Copilot reviewed 11 out of 11 changed files in this pull request and generated 2 comments.

<details>
<summary>Show a summary per file</summary>

| File | Description |
| ---- | ----------- |
| packages/dds/tree/src/shared-tree/treeAlpha.ts | Core implementation of ParentObject types, parent2(), on(), and updated child()/children() methods with caching |
| packages/dds/tree/src/simple-tree/core/treeNodeKernel.ts | Added status event tracking mechanism with #statusEvents emitter and checkAndEmitStatusChange() |
| packages/dds/tree/src/test/shared-tree/tree.spec.ts | Comprehensive tests for parent2(), on(), child(), children() with all ParentObject types and edge cases |
| packages/dds/tree/src/simple-tree/api/treeNodeApi.ts | Updated TSDoc links to use numbered overload syntax for child() and children() |
| packages/dds/tree/src/test/simple-tree/api/treeNodeApi.spec.ts | Updated Map type declarations to include undefined in key type for children() |
| packages/framework/fluid-framework/api-report/fluid-framework.alpha.api.md | Public API surface additions for ParentObject, ParentType, TreeNodeParent types and new TreeAlpha methods |
| packages/dds/tree/api-report/tree.alpha.api.md | Alpha API surface additions matching framework changes |
| packages/dds/tree/src/simple-tree/index.ts | Export UnhydratedFlexTreeNode type for internal use |
| packages/dds/tree/src/simple-tree/core/index.ts | Export KernelStatusEvents and StatusChangedEventData |
| packages/dds/tree/src/shared-tree/index.ts | Re-export ParentObject-related types |
| packages/dds/tree/src/index.ts | Re-export ParentObject-related types at top level |
</details>

pr_title: Add ParentObject type used in new TreeAlpha.on method
body:
You want any branch to be usable as this type. Using `TreeView<ImplicitFieldSchema> ` has two distinct issues:

1. TreeView is invariant over its schema, not covariant, so it will be impossible to actually use this for a view with any schema that is not exactly `ImplicitFieldSchema`. Well, it might work as the write type for the root ends up being never which might happen to accidently work if TS checks is structurally, but its not supposed to work. (see https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Type_variance )
2. It won't work for branches which are not the main view.

You want to use `TreeBranch` instead.

pr_title: Add ParentObject type used in new TreeAlpha.on method
body:
I don't think "ParentObject" is a great name as it does not convey what its a parent of, and sating something is an object also isn't very useful here (the fact that its an object is not important, and could be confused woth object nodes).

I think TreeParent would be a better name, as it indicates its a parent of a tree, and also fits in well with names like TreeNode and treeChanged

TreeNodeParent would be another option.

pr_title: Add ParentObject type used in new TreeAlpha.on method
body:
This should have a doc comment noting the semantics of this type. For example that for nodes they are the parents of all their immediate children (all their fields's contents) and for branches they are the parent of the document root node.

It should clarify what the keys are for the special cases (branches) and how removed and unhydrated nodes are handled. Assuming we plan to add support for them later, we likley will want a disclaimer on this type (as private remakrs) noting more values can be added to it and this should be done before stabilizing it.

+# SharedTree Eventing System Enhancements
+
+# Summary
+
+The SharedTree eventing layer currently provides great, but limited APIs for observing state changes. This document proposes several enhancements to the SharedTree eventing system in the Fluid Framework, as well as covering some of its current limitations. 
+
+These enhancements aim to improve:
+- Consistent, ergonomic root-level and document-level change events.
+- Diff payloads for nodeChanged and treeChanged events
+- Subtree diff representations (Sparse, Flat, Aggregate Summary)
+- Support for detached tree events
+
+---
+
+## Current State
+We currently have many different tree related event emitters from different layers of the tree. Here’s a summary of our APIs.
+
+| Layer      | API                             | Trigger                                             | Example Use-case                                      |
+|------------|----------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------|
+| View       | `rootChanged`                    | Root field reassigned / schema change               | Handle schema updates, or reset root                  |
+| View       | `schemaChanged`                  | Stored schema changed                                | Ensure type-dependent tree contents, re-check compatibility |
+| View       | `commitApplied`                  | Local commit landed / transaction committed          | Wire undo/redo via revertible                          |
+| TreeBeta   | `nodeChanged`                    | Node’s direct fields/values changed                  | Check which specific field changed via `changedProperties` |
+| TreeBeta   | `treeChanged`                    | Subtree changed                                      | Detect updates under a subtree                         |
+| Flex-tree  | `nodeChanging` / `subtreeChanging` | Node or subtree is in process of change           | Detect node/subtree changes before applying            |
+| Flex-tree  | `nodeChanged` / `treeChanged`     | Node or subtree changed                            | Detect applicable updates                              |
+| TreeAlpha  | `trackObservations` / `trackObservationsOnce` | Change tracking | Invalidation-based processing (React re-render) |
+| Checkout   | `beforeBatch`                    | Before a group of edits is applied                   | Tree may be unstable                                   |
+| Checkout   | `afterBatch`                     | After group of edits is applied                      | Tree stable again                                      |
+
+---
+
+# Proposed Enhancements
+
+---
+
+## 1. Root Events: `rootNodeChanged` / `rootTreeChanged`
+
+### Problem
+SharedTree currently has three different events which are used in different combinations to cover the different use cases for observing root / whole document level changes.
+- nodeChanged(node) – direct field mutations of the node
+- treeChanged(node) – any mutations in the subtree under the node
+- rootChanged – replacement under the root field (new root node)
+
+Currently there is no single API that covers root field level mutations or “whole document” changes. Instead, our current way to do this are the following.
+
+### Root field level mutations**

body:
I don't think `resubscribeOnRootReplace` makes sense here. These events are not subscribing to the root _node_. They are subscribing to the root _field_, which is parented by the _view_. The root node can come and go, but the view does not change. So if you register `rootNodeChanged` or `rootTreeChanged`, those events will never go away until you deregister them. If you replace the root node, they still don't go away. There's no replacement that could make them go away, so the flag doesn't do anything.

`resubscribeOnReplace` makes sense on all the _other_ events - `Tree.on(...)` because they are associated with _nodes_. If the _node_ that the event is registered on gets replaced, then a flag like this could help avoid the annoying "I gotta watch the parent too" logic. It's an interesting idea, however:

1. It could probably be written as a helper/wrapper rather than a flag, which might be preferable.
2. It's unclear what happens if the node is removed - not replaced - but then another node comes back in the same place later. Is that still considered a replace?
3. Suppose we add event parameters that are specific to the node type (as is proposed elsewhere in this document). For example, array nodes provide information about the indexes that moved/changed, or map nodes provide the keys that were added/removed/changed. Then, what happens if you subscribe to an array node with `resubscribeOnReplace` but then the array node gets replaced with a map node? Would we fire the event but without supplying the index information? Would we skip firing the event? Is it effectively deregistered, or does it come back if the map node is then later replaced with an array node?

+# SharedTree Eventing System Enhancements
+
+# Summary
+
+The SharedTree eventing layer currently provides great, but limited APIs for observing state changes. This document proposes several enhancements to the SharedTree eventing system in the Fluid Framework, as well as covering some of its current limitations. 
+
+These enhancements aim to improve:
+- Consistent, ergonomic root-level and document-level change events.
+- Diff payloads for nodeChanged and treeChanged events
+- Subtree diff representations (Sparse, Flat, Aggregate Summary)
+- Support for detached tree events
+
+---
+
+## Current State
+We currently have many different tree related event emitters from different layers of the tree. Here’s a summary of our APIs.
+
+| Layer      | API                             | Trigger                                             | Example Use-case                                      |
+|------------|----------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------|
+| View       | `rootChanged`                    | Root field reassigned / schema change               | Handle schema updates, or reset root                  |
+| View       | `schemaChanged`                  | Stored schema changed                                | Ensure type-dependent tree contents, re-check compatibility |
+| View       | `commitApplied`                  | Local commit landed / transaction committed          | Wire undo/redo via revertible                          |
+| TreeBeta   | `nodeChanged`                    | Node’s direct fields/values changed                  | Check which specific field changed via `changedProperties` |
+| TreeBeta   | `treeChanged`                    | Subtree changed                                      | Detect updates under a subtree                         |
+| Flex-tree  | `nodeChanging` / `subtreeChanging` | Node or subtree is in process of change           | Detect node/subtree changes before applying            |
+| Flex-tree  | `nodeChanged` / `treeChanged`     | Node or subtree changed                            | Detect applicable updates                              |
+| TreeAlpha  | `trackObservations` / `trackObservationsOnce` | Change tracking | Invalidation-based processing (React re-render) |
+| Checkout   | `beforeBatch`                    | Before a group of edits is applied                   | Tree may be unstable                                   |
+| Checkout   | `afterBatch`                     | After group of edits is applied                      | Tree stable again                                      |
+
+---
+
+# Proposed Enhancements
+
+---
+
+## 1. Root Events: `rootNodeChanged` / `rootTreeChanged`
+
+### Problem
+SharedTree currently has three different events which are used in different combinations to cover the different use cases for observing root / whole document level changes.
+- nodeChanged(node) – direct field mutations of the node
+- treeChanged(node) – any mutations in the subtree under the node
+- rootChanged – replacement under the root field (new root node)
+
+Currently there is no single API that covers root field level mutations or “whole document” changes. Instead, our current way to do this are the following.
+
+### Root field level mutations**
+- Listen to “rootChanged”
+- Subscribe to nodeChanged(rootNode)
+-  If “rootChanged” event fires, re-subscribe to nodeChanged(newRootNode)
+
+### Whole document changed**
+- Listen to “rootChanged”
+- Subscribe to treeChanged(rootNode)
+- If “rootChanged” fires, re-subscribe to treeChanged(newRootNode)
+
+Additionally, the “rootChanged” event takes in a view (as it does not have a parent node) and triggers for schema changes (which we may or may not want to separate). This makes subscribing to root level events feel inconsistent and less ergonomic compared to other events.
+
+### Proposal
+
+Introduce root level wrapper events which behave like their node-level counterparts which automatically tracks root replacements for resubscribing. These wrapper events do not introduce any new capabilities but only exist to unify the eventing APIs for root nodes vs non-root nodes.
+
+### Potential Solution
+
+#### rootNodeChanged/rootTreeChanged
+These APIs would mirror nodeChanged, but can optionally omit schema changes, and can automatically:
+- Detect root replacement
+- Unsubscribe to old root
+- Resubscribe to new root
+
+```ts
+treeView.events.on(“rootNodeChanged”, listener, {
+resubscribeOnRootReplace: boolean;

body:
Have you researched what the cost would be to provide a "node snapshot"? Even for just the shallow `"nodeChanged"` event, you'd need to snapshot the entire subtree under the registered node (so the user can walk down it). You could even argue that you need to snapshot the entire tree, because the user might walk _up_ with `Tree.parent`, though, we could probably reasonably restrict that if we needed to.

1. What's the development cost of this - how hard is it to keep around a snapshot?
2. What's the performance cost of this? I suspect it's cheaper if using a ChunkedForest but potentially expensive if using an ObjectForest, though I can't say for sure.

Yann might be a good resource to answer these questions.

+# SharedTree Eventing System Enhancements
+
+# Summary
+
+The SharedTree eventing layer currently provides great, but limited APIs for observing state changes. This document proposes several enhancements to the SharedTree eventing system in the Fluid Framework, as well as covering some of its current limitations. 
+
+These enhancements aim to improve:
+- Consistent, ergonomic root-level and document-level change events.
+- Diff payloads for nodeChanged and treeChanged events
+- Subtree diff representations (Sparse, Flat, Aggregate Summary)
+- Support for detached tree events
+
+---
+
+## Current State
+We currently have many different tree related event emitters from different layers of the tree. Here’s a summary of our APIs.
+
+| Layer      | API                             | Trigger                                             | Example Use-case                                      |
+|------------|----------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------|
+| View       | `rootChanged`                    | Root field reassigned / schema change               | Handle schema updates, or reset root                  |
+| View       | `schemaChanged`                  | Stored schema changed                                | Ensure type-dependent tree contents, re-check compatibility |
+| View       | `commitApplied`                  | Local commit landed / transaction committed          | Wire undo/redo via revertible                          |
+| TreeBeta   | `nodeChanged`                    | Node’s direct fields/values changed                  | Check which specific field changed via `changedProperties` |
+| TreeBeta   | `treeChanged`                    | Subtree changed                                      | Detect updates under a subtree                         |
+| Flex-tree  | `nodeChanging` / `subtreeChanging` | Node or subtree is in process of change           | Detect node/subtree changes before applying            |
+| Flex-tree  | `nodeChanged` / `treeChanged`     | Node or subtree changed                            | Detect applicable updates                              |
+| TreeAlpha  | `trackObservations` / `trackObservationsOnce` | Change tracking | Invalidation-based processing (React re-render) |
+| Checkout   | `beforeBatch`                    | Before a group of edits is applied                   | Tree may be unstable                                   |
+| Checkout   | `afterBatch`                     | After group of edits is applied                      | Tree stable again                                      |
+
+---
+
+# Proposed Enhancements
+
+---
+
+## 1. Root Events: `rootNodeChanged` / `rootTreeChanged`
+
+### Problem
+SharedTree currently has three different events which are used in different combinations to cover the different use cases for observing root / whole document level changes.
+- nodeChanged(node) – direct field mutations of the node
+- treeChanged(node) – any mutations in the subtree under the node
+- rootChanged – replacement under the root field (new root node)
+
+Currently there is no single API that covers root field level mutations or “whole document” changes. Instead, our current way to do this are the following.
+
+### Root field level mutations**
+- Listen to “rootChanged”
+- Subscribe to nodeChanged(rootNode)
+-  If “rootChanged” event fires, re-subscribe to nodeChanged(newRootNode)
+
+### Whole document changed**
+- Listen to “rootChanged”
+- Subscribe to treeChanged(rootNode)
+- If “rootChanged” fires, re-subscribe to treeChanged(newRootNode)
+
+Additionally, the “rootChanged” event takes in a view (as it does not have a parent node) and triggers for schema changes (which we may or may not want to separate). This makes subscribing to root level events feel inconsistent and less ergonomic compared to other events.
+
+### Proposal
+
+Introduce root level wrapper events which behave like their node-level counterparts which automatically tracks root replacements for resubscribing. These wrapper events do not introduce any new capabilities but only exist to unify the eventing APIs for root nodes vs non-root nodes.
+
+### Potential Solution
+
+#### rootNodeChanged/rootTreeChanged
+These APIs would mirror nodeChanged, but can optionally omit schema changes, and can automatically:
+- Detect root replacement
+- Unsubscribe to old root
+- Resubscribe to new root
+
+```ts
+treeView.events.on(“rootNodeChanged”, listener, {
+resubscribeOnRootReplace: boolean;
+includeSchemaChanges?: boolean;
+});
+```
+
+```ts
+treeView.events.on(“rootTreeChanged”, listener, {
+resubscribeOnRootReplace: boolean;
+includeSchemaChanges?: boolean;
+});
+```
+
+These APIs would eliminate some of the boilerplate from our current workflow and would make the eventing model more consistent between the root and all other nodes. 
+
+| Operation                           | rootChanged | rootNodeChanged | rootTreeChanged |
+|-------------------------------------|-------------|------------------|------------------|
+| Replace root pointer                | Yes         | Yes*             | Yes*             |
+| Insert/delete/move direct child     | No          | Yes              | Yes              |
+| Insert/delete/move anywhere         | No          | No               | Yes              |
+| Mutate value/field on root          | No          | Yes              | Yes              |
+| Schema change                       | Yes         | Optional         | Optional         |
+
+\*when `resubscribeOnRootReplace = true`
+
+Note: When includeSchemaChanges is false, rootNodeChanged/rootTreeChanged fires only for structural or field-level changes, not schema-only changes. 
+
+---
+
+## 2. Diff Payload for nodeChanged 
+
+### Problem
+
+Our event emitters currently tell us whether the node or tree has changed, but it does not specify what specific operation took place. For example, if we emit an event on an arrayNode that underwent an insert operation, it may be useful to know which index the insert occurred at (or what was inserted).
+
+### Proposal
+
+---
+
+#### Option A: Previous Tree State Payload
+
+Provide the previous tree state in the event payload. As this feature is something that is requested by a few select customers, it may not be very widely used. If this is the case, simply providing a “node snapshot” of the previous tree state, and letting the users decide what to do would be the easiest way.

body:
I expect we'd have to clone the forest (which is indeed cheap on ChunkedForest but potentially expensive if using an ObjectForest) and keep an anchor to the node in the cloned forest.
This is probably not hard to implement (Craig would know best).

It does seems like a questionable API choice since comparing states is a bit error prone (especially when considering the maintenance burden of the code doing the comparison), and it is lossy (e.g., not clear what was moved where or what was changed).
I'm not saying I couldn't be convinced, but I would want to learn more about the app scenario to see if we can offer something better.

+# SharedTree Eventing System Enhancements
+
+# Summary
+
+The SharedTree eventing layer currently provides great, but limited APIs for observing state changes. This document proposes several enhancements to the SharedTree eventing system in the Fluid Framework, as well as covering some of its current limitations. 
+
+These enhancements aim to improve:
+- Consistent, ergonomic root-level and document-level change events.
+- Diff payloads for nodeChanged and treeChanged events
+- Subtree diff representations (Sparse, Flat, Aggregate Summary)
+- Support for detached tree events
+
+---
+
+## Current State
+We currently have many different tree related event emitters from different layers of the tree. Here’s a summary of our APIs.
+
+| Layer      | API                             | Trigger                                             | Example Use-case                                      |
+|------------|----------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------|
+| View       | `rootChanged`                    | Root field reassigned / schema change               | Handle schema updates, or reset root                  |
+| View       | `schemaChanged`                  | Stored schema changed                                | Ensure type-dependent tree contents, re-check compatibility |
+| View       | `commitApplied`                  | Local commit landed / transaction committed          | Wire undo/redo via revertible                          |
+| TreeBeta   | `nodeChanged`                    | Node’s direct fields/values changed                  | Check which specific field changed via `changedProperties` |
+| TreeBeta   | `treeChanged`                    | Subtree changed                                      | Detect updates under a subtree                         |
+| Flex-tree  | `nodeChanging` / `subtreeChanging` | Node or subtree is in process of change           | Detect node/subtree changes before applying            |
+| Flex-tree  | `nodeChanged` / `treeChanged`     | Node or subtree changed                            | Detect applicable updates                              |
+| TreeAlpha  | `trackObservations` / `trackObservationsOnce` | Change tracking | Invalidation-based processing (React re-render) |
+| Checkout   | `beforeBatch`                    | Before a group of edits is applied                   | Tree may be unstable                                   |
+| Checkout   | `afterBatch`                     | After group of edits is applied                      | Tree stable again                                      |
+
+---
+
+# Proposed Enhancements
+
+---
+
+## 1. Root Events: `rootNodeChanged` / `rootTreeChanged`
+
+### Problem
+SharedTree currently has three different events which are used in different combinations to cover the different use cases for observing root / whole document level changes.
+- nodeChanged(node) – direct field mutations of the node
+- treeChanged(node) – any mutations in the subtree under the node
+- rootChanged – replacement under the root field (new root node)
+
+Currently there is no single API that covers root field level mutations or “whole document” changes. Instead, our current way to do this are the following.
+
+### Root field level mutations**
+- Listen to “rootChanged”
+- Subscribe to nodeChanged(rootNode)
+-  If “rootChanged” event fires, re-subscribe to nodeChanged(newRootNode)
+
+### Whole document changed**
+- Listen to “rootChanged”
+- Subscribe to treeChanged(rootNode)
+- If “rootChanged” fires, re-subscribe to treeChanged(newRootNode)
+
+Additionally, the “rootChanged” event takes in a view (as it does not have a parent node) and triggers for schema changes (which we may or may not want to separate). This makes subscribing to root level events feel inconsistent and less ergonomic compared to other events.
+
+### Proposal
+
+Introduce root level wrapper events which behave like their node-level counterparts which automatically tracks root replacements for resubscribing. These wrapper events do not introduce any new capabilities but only exist to unify the eventing APIs for root nodes vs non-root nodes.
+
+### Potential Solution
+
+#### rootNodeChanged/rootTreeChanged
+These APIs would mirror nodeChanged, but can optionally omit schema changes, and can automatically:
+- Detect root replacement
+- Unsubscribe to old root
+- Resubscribe to new root
+
+```ts
+treeView.events.on(“rootNodeChanged”, listener, {
+resubscribeOnRootReplace: boolean;
+includeSchemaChanges?: boolean;
+});
+```
+
+```ts
+treeView.events.on(“rootTreeChanged”, listener, {
+resubscribeOnRootReplace: boolean;
+includeSchemaChanges?: boolean;
+});
+```
+
+These APIs would eliminate some of the boilerplate from our current workflow and would make the eventing model more consistent between the root and all other nodes. 
+
+| Operation                           | rootChanged | rootNodeChanged | rootTreeChanged |
+|-------------------------------------|-------------|------------------|------------------|
+| Replace root pointer                | Yes         | Yes*             | Yes*             |
+| Insert/delete/move direct child     | No          | Yes              | Yes              |
+| Insert/delete/move anywhere         | No          | No               | Yes              |
+| Mutate value/field on root          | No          | Yes              | Yes              |
+| Schema change                       | Yes         | Optional         | Optional         |
+
+\*when `resubscribeOnRootReplace = true`
+
+Note: When includeSchemaChanges is false, rootNodeChanged/rootTreeChanged fires only for structural or field-level changes, not schema-only changes. 
+
+---
+
+## 2. Diff Payload for nodeChanged 
+
+### Problem
+
+Our event emitters currently tell us whether the node or tree has changed, but it does not specify what specific operation took place. For example, if we emit an event on an arrayNode that underwent an insert operation, it may be useful to know which index the insert occurred at (or what was inserted).
+
+### Proposal
+
+---
+
+#### Option A: Previous Tree State Payload
+
+Provide the previous tree state in the event payload. As this feature is something that is requested by a few select customers, it may not be very widely used. If this is the case, simply providing a “node snapshot” of the previous tree state, and letting the users decide what to do would be the easiest way.
+
+We may consider the following to represent the previous tree state:
+	- JsonableTree representation of node/subtree
+	- shallow structural clone
+
+```ts
+type NodeSnapshot = unknown // exact type TBD
+
+type NodeChangedEvent = {
+	node: NodeHandle;
+	changedProperties: Set<string>;
+	getPreviousState?: () => NodeSnapshot | undefined
+}
+```
+
+---
+
+#### Non-goal: Exposing Raw deltas
+
+Although exposing the raw deltas may be just as easy to implement (while providing all the information we need), they are too difficult to read/interpret correctly for it to be a viable solution.
+
+---
+
+#### Option B: Normalized Diff Payload
+
+Expose an optional getDiff() function when subscribing to a nodeChanged event, exposing a read friendly, structured and versioned diff payload.
+
+##### Potential Solution
+
+Deltas stores edit information and maybe can be used to provide the diffs for the specific node we’re interested in. However, the information provided from a delta is not always isolated to the specific node, and the format of the deltas is difficult to read and understand.
+
+One way to implement this would be to create some sort of delta transformer function to isolate the relevant parts of the delta that we need and converts it to a read-friendly format. This data can then be sent through the payload when emitting the event. A stable versioned schema for diff payloads should also be considered to decouple it from our internal change formats.
+
+The existing trackDirtyNodes function can be leveraged to determine which nodes changed during the batch so that the delta transformer only computes the diffs for those nodes.
+
+We should also compute diffs once per transaction and expose them lazily or behind an `{includeDelta: true}` flag to avoid payload bloat. 
+
+```ts
+type DiffV1 = 
+	| {kind: “insert”; index: number; content: NodeHandle[]}
+	| {kind: “delete”; index: number; count: number}
+	| {kind: “move”; srcIndex: number; count: number; dstIndex: number}
+	| {kind: “set”; oldValue: unknown; newValue: unknown }
+interface ReadableDiffV1 {
+	version: 1;
+	node: NodeHandle;
+	fields?: Record<FieldKey, DiffV1[]>;

body: One thing that seems relevant to this question: while the change operations that are communicated to the `DeltaVisitor` are the same shape no matter the kind of containing field/node (they're just series of `detach` and `attach` operations in the field), these should be interpreted slightly differently depending on the containing field/node: a pair of detach & attach in an object/map/record field means the old content has been replaced. The same thing in an array field is not a true replacement because the detached and attached content occupy different cells in the sequence.

body:
When it comes to diffs on array nodes, it would be nice if what we settle on didn't need dramatic changes when we start emitting change events for rich text content. IOW, it would be nice if the change events for text were the same as for normal arrays, but with added stuff. We don't have a design for what that will look like yet, but we do have a pretty meaningful requirement: our change events for text should be convertible into the Quill.JS `Delta` format. Here are some relevant links:
* https://quilljs.com/docs/delta
* https://github.com/slab/delta
* https://quilljs.com/docs/guides/designing-the-delta-format

+# SharedTree Eventing System Enhancements
+
+# Summary
+
+The SharedTree eventing layer currently provides great, but limited APIs for observing state changes. This document proposes several enhancements to the SharedTree eventing system in the Fluid Framework, as well as covering some of its current limitations. 
+
+These enhancements aim to improve:
+- Consistent, ergonomic root-level and document-level change events.
+- Diff payloads for nodeChanged and treeChanged events
+- Subtree diff representations (Sparse, Flat, Aggregate Summary)
+- Support for detached tree events
+
+---
+
+## Current State
+We currently have many different tree related event emitters from different layers of the tree. Here’s a summary of our APIs.
+
+| Layer      | API                             | Trigger                                             | Example Use-case                                      |
+|------------|----------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------|
+| View       | `rootChanged`                    | Root field reassigned / schema change               | Handle schema updates, or reset root                  |
+| View       | `schemaChanged`                  | Stored schema changed                                | Ensure type-dependent tree contents, re-check compatibility |
+| View       | `commitApplied`                  | Local commit landed / transaction committed          | Wire undo/redo via revertible                          |
+| TreeBeta   | `nodeChanged`                    | Node’s direct fields/values changed                  | Check which specific field changed via `changedProperties` |
+| TreeBeta   | `treeChanged`                    | Subtree changed                                      | Detect updates under a subtree                         |
+| Flex-tree  | `nodeChanging` / `subtreeChanging` | Node or subtree is in process of change           | Detect node/subtree changes before applying            |
+| Flex-tree  | `nodeChanged` / `treeChanged`     | Node or subtree changed                            | Detect applicable updates                              |
+| TreeAlpha  | `trackObservations` / `trackObservationsOnce` | Change tracking | Invalidation-based processing (React re-render) |
+| Checkout   | `beforeBatch`                    | Before a group of edits is applied                   | Tree may be unstable                                   |
+| Checkout   | `afterBatch`                     | After group of edits is applied                      | Tree stable again                                      |
+
+---
+
+# Proposed Enhancements
+
+---
+
+## 1. Root Events: `rootNodeChanged` / `rootTreeChanged`
+
+### Problem
+SharedTree currently has three different events which are used in different combinations to cover the different use cases for observing root / whole document level changes.
+- nodeChanged(node) – direct field mutations of the node
+- treeChanged(node) – any mutations in the subtree under the node
+- rootChanged – replacement under the root field (new root node)
+
+Currently there is no single API that covers root field level mutations or “whole document” changes. Instead, our current way to do this are the following.
+
+### Root field level mutations**
+- Listen to “rootChanged”
+- Subscribe to nodeChanged(rootNode)
+-  If “rootChanged” event fires, re-subscribe to nodeChanged(newRootNode)
+
+### Whole document changed**
+- Listen to “rootChanged”
+- Subscribe to treeChanged(rootNode)
+- If “rootChanged” fires, re-subscribe to treeChanged(newRootNode)
+
+Additionally, the “rootChanged” event takes in a view (as it does not have a parent node) and triggers for schema changes (which we may or may not want to separate). This makes subscribing to root level events feel inconsistent and less ergonomic compared to other events.
+
+### Proposal
+
+Introduce root level wrapper events which behave like their node-level counterparts which automatically tracks root replacements for resubscribing. These wrapper events do not introduce any new capabilities but only exist to unify the eventing APIs for root nodes vs non-root nodes.
+
+### Potential Solution
+
+#### rootNodeChanged/rootTreeChanged
+These APIs would mirror nodeChanged, but can optionally omit schema changes, and can automatically:
+- Detect root replacement
+- Unsubscribe to old root
+- Resubscribe to new root
+
+```ts
+treeView.events.on(“rootNodeChanged”, listener, {
+resubscribeOnRootReplace: boolean;
+includeSchemaChanges?: boolean;
+});
+```
+
+```ts
+treeView.events.on(“rootTreeChanged”, listener, {
+resubscribeOnRootReplace: boolean;
+includeSchemaChanges?: boolean;
+});
+```
+
+These APIs would eliminate some of the boilerplate from our current workflow and would make the eventing model more consistent between the root and all other nodes. 
+
+| Operation                           | rootChanged | rootNodeChanged | rootTreeChanged |
+|-------------------------------------|-------------|------------------|------------------|
+| Replace root pointer                | Yes         | Yes*             | Yes*             |
+| Insert/delete/move direct child     | No          | Yes              | Yes              |
+| Insert/delete/move anywhere         | No          | No               | Yes              |
+| Mutate value/field on root          | No          | Yes              | Yes              |
+| Schema change                       | Yes         | Optional         | Optional         |
+
+\*when `resubscribeOnRootReplace = true`
+
+Note: When includeSchemaChanges is false, rootNodeChanged/rootTreeChanged fires only for structural or field-level changes, not schema-only changes. 
+
+---
+
+## 2. Diff Payload for nodeChanged 
+
+### Problem
+
+Our event emitters currently tell us whether the node or tree has changed, but it does not specify what specific operation took place. For example, if we emit an event on an arrayNode that underwent an insert operation, it may be useful to know which index the insert occurred at (or what was inserted).
+
+### Proposal
+
+---
+
+#### Option A: Previous Tree State Payload
+
+Provide the previous tree state in the event payload. As this feature is something that is requested by a few select customers, it may not be very widely used. If this is the case, simply providing a “node snapshot” of the previous tree state, and letting the users decide what to do would be the easiest way.
+
+We may consider the following to represent the previous tree state:
+	- JsonableTree representation of node/subtree
+	- shallow structural clone
+
+```ts
+type NodeSnapshot = unknown // exact type TBD
+
+type NodeChangedEvent = {
+	node: NodeHandle;
+	changedProperties: Set<string>;
+	getPreviousState?: () => NodeSnapshot | undefined
+}
+```
+
+---
+
+#### Non-goal: Exposing Raw deltas
+
+Although exposing the raw deltas may be just as easy to implement (while providing all the information we need), they are too difficult to read/interpret correctly for it to be a viable solution.
+
+---
+
+#### Option B: Normalized Diff Payload
+
+Expose an optional getDiff() function when subscribing to a nodeChanged event, exposing a read friendly, structured and versioned diff payload.
+
+##### Potential Solution
+
+Deltas stores edit information and maybe can be used to provide the diffs for the specific node we’re interested in. However, the information provided from a delta is not always isolated to the specific node, and the format of the deltas is difficult to read and understand.
+
+One way to implement this would be to create some sort of delta transformer function to isolate the relevant parts of the delta that we need and converts it to a read-friendly format. This data can then be sent through the payload when emitting the event. A stable versioned schema for diff payloads should also be considered to decouple it from our internal change formats.
+
+The existing trackDirtyNodes function can be leveraged to determine which nodes changed during the batch so that the delta transformer only computes the diffs for those nodes.
+
+We should also compute diffs once per transaction and expose them lazily or behind an `{includeDelta: true}` flag to avoid payload bloat. 
+
+```ts
+type DiffV1 = 
+	| {kind: “insert”; index: number; content: NodeHandle[]}
+	| {kind: “delete”; index: number; count: number}
+	| {kind: “move”; srcIndex: number; count: number; dstIndex: number}
+	| {kind: “set”; oldValue: unknown; newValue: unknown }

+| TreeBeta   | `treeChanged`                    | Subtree changed                                      | Detect updates under a subtree                         |
+| Flex-tree  | `nodeChanging` / `subtreeChanging` | Node or subtree is in process of change           | Detect node/subtree changes before applying            |
+| Flex-tree  | `nodeChanged` / `treeChanged`     | Node or subtree changed                            | Detect applicable updates                              |
+| TreeAlpha  | `trackObservations` / `trackObservationsOnce` | Change tracking | Invalidation-based processing (React re-render) |
+| Checkout   | `beforeBatch`                    | Before a group of edits is applied                   | Tree may be unstable                                   |
+| Checkout   | `afterBatch`                     | After group of edits is applied                      | Tree stable again                                      |
+
+---
+
+# Proposed Enhancements
+
+---
+
+## 1. Root Events: `rootNodeChanged` / `rootTreeChanged`
+
+### Problem
+SharedTree currently has three different events which are used in different combinations to cover the different use cases for observing root / whole document level changes.
+- nodeChanged(node) – direct field mutations of the node
+- treeChanged(node) – any mutations in the subtree under the node
+- rootChanged – replacement under the root field (new root node)
+
+Currently there is no single API that covers root field level mutations or “whole document” changes. Instead, our current way to do this are the following.
+
+### Root field level mutations**
+- Listen to “rootChanged”
+- Subscribe to nodeChanged(rootNode)
+-  If “rootChanged” event fires, re-subscribe to nodeChanged(newRootNode)
+
+### Whole document changed**
+- Listen to “rootChanged”
+- Subscribe to treeChanged(rootNode)
+- If “rootChanged” fires, re-subscribe to treeChanged(newRootNode)
+
+Additionally, the “rootChanged” event takes in a view (as it does not have a parent node) and triggers for schema changes (which we may or may not want to separate). This makes subscribing to root level events feel inconsistent and less ergonomic compared to other events.
+
+### Proposal
+
+Introduce root level wrapper events which behave like their node-level counterparts which automatically tracks root replacements for resubscribing. These wrapper events do not introduce any new capabilities but only exist to unify the eventing APIs for root nodes vs non-root nodes.
+
+### Potential Solution
+
+#### rootNodeChanged/rootTreeChanged
+These APIs would mirror nodeChanged, but can optionally omit schema changes, and can automatically:
+- Detect root replacement
+- Unsubscribe to old root
+- Resubscribe to new root
+
+```ts
+treeView.events.on(“rootNodeChanged”, listener, {
+resubscribeOnRootReplace: boolean;
+includeSchemaChanges?: boolean;
+});
+```
+
+```ts
+treeView.events.on(“rootTreeChanged”, listener, {
+resubscribeOnRootReplace: boolean;
+includeSchemaChanges?: boolean;
+});
+```
+
+These APIs would eliminate some of the boilerplate from our current workflow and would make the eventing model more consistent between the root and all other nodes. 
+
+| Operation                           | rootChanged | rootNodeChanged | rootTreeChanged |
+|-------------------------------------|-------------|------------------|------------------|
+| Replace root pointer                | Yes         | Yes*             | Yes*             |
+| Insert/delete/move direct child     | No          | Yes              | Yes              |
+| Insert/delete/move anywhere         | No          | No               | Yes              |
+| Mutate value/field on root          | No          | Yes              | Yes              |
+| Schema change                       | Yes         | Optional         | Optional         |
+
+\*when `resubscribeOnRootReplace = true`
+
+Note: When includeSchemaChanges is false, rootNodeChanged/rootTreeChanged fires only for structural or field-level changes, not schema-only changes. 
+
+---
+
+## 2. Diff Payload for nodeChanged 
+
+### Problem
+
+Our event emitters currently tell us whether the node or tree has changed, but it does not specify what specific operation took place. For example, if we emit an event on an arrayNode that underwent an insert operation, it may be useful to know which index the insert occurred at (or what was inserted).
+
+### Proposal
+
+---
+
+#### Option A: Previous Tree State Payload
+
+Provide the previous tree state in the event payload. As this feature is something that is requested by a few select customers, it may not be very widely used. If this is the case, simply providing a “node snapshot” of the previous tree state, and letting the users decide what to do would be the easiest way.
+
+We may consider the following to represent the previous tree state:
+	- JsonableTree representation of node/subtree
+	- shallow structural clone
+
+```ts
+type NodeSnapshot = unknown // exact type TBD
+
+type NodeChangedEvent = {
+	node: NodeHandle;
+	changedProperties: Set<string>;
+	getPreviousState?: () => NodeSnapshot | undefined
+}
+```
+
+---
+
+#### Non-goal: Exposing Raw deltas
+
+Although exposing the raw deltas may be just as easy to implement (while providing all the information we need), they are too difficult to read/interpret correctly for it to be a viable solution.
+
+---
+
+#### Option B: Normalized Diff Payload
+
+Expose an optional getDiff() function when subscribing to a nodeChanged event, exposing a read friendly, structured and versioned diff payload.
+
+##### Potential Solution
+
+Deltas stores edit information and maybe can be used to provide the diffs for the specific node we’re interested in. However, the information provided from a delta is not always isolated to the specific node, and the format of the deltas is difficult to read and understand.
+
+One way to implement this would be to create some sort of delta transformer function to isolate the relevant parts of the delta that we need and converts it to a read-friendly format. This data can then be sent through the payload when emitting the event. A stable versioned schema for diff payloads should also be considered to decouple it from our internal change formats.
+
+The existing trackDirtyNodes function can be leveraged to determine which nodes changed during the batch so that the delta transformer only computes the diffs for those nodes.
+
+We should also compute diffs once per transaction and expose them lazily or behind an `{includeDelta: true}` flag to avoid payload bloat. 
+
+```ts
+type DiffV1 = 
+	| {kind: “insert”; index: number; content: NodeHandle[]}
+	| {kind: “delete”; index: number; count: number}
+	| {kind: “move”; srcIndex: number; count: number; dstIndex: number}
+	| {kind: “set”; oldValue: unknown; newValue: unknown }
+interface ReadableDiffV1 {
+	version: 1;
+	node: NodeHandle;
+	fields?: Record<FieldKey, DiffV1[]>;
+	truncated?: boolean;
+	meta?: {origin: “local” | “remote”, revision: RevisionTag} // unsure if this is necessary

+# SharedTree Eventing System Enhancements
+
+# Summary
+
+The SharedTree eventing layer currently provides great, but limited APIs for observing state changes. This document proposes several enhancements to the SharedTree eventing system in the Fluid Framework, as well as covering some of its current limitations.

+- Consistent, ergonomic root-level and document-level change events.
+- Diff payloads for nodeChanged and treeChanged events

+

+We currently have many different tree related event emitters from different layers of the tree. Here’s a summary of our APIs.
+
+| Layer      | API                             | Trigger                                             | Example Use-case                                      |

+| View       | `rootChanged`                    | Root field reassigned / schema change               | Handle schema updates, or reset root                  |

+| Flex-tree  | `nodeChanged` / `treeChanged`     | Node or subtree changed                            | Detect applicable updates                              |
+| TreeAlpha  | `trackObservations` / `trackObservationsOnce` | Change tracking | Invalidation-based processing (React re-render) |
+| Checkout   | `beforeBatch`                    | Before a group of edits is applied                   | Tree may be unstable                                   |
+| Checkout   | `afterBatch`                     | After group of edits is applied                      | Tree stable again                                      |

+});

+});

+|-------------------------------------|-------------|------------------|------------------|

+| Insert/delete/move anywhere         | No          | No               | Yes              |
+| Mutate value/field on root          | No          | Yes              | Yes              |

+
+\*when `resubscribeOnRootReplace = true`
+
+Note: When includeSchemaChanges is false, rootNodeChanged/rootTreeChanged fires only for structural or field-level changes, not schema-only changes. 
+
+---
+
+## 2. Diff Payload for nodeChanged 
+

+
+We may consider the following to represent the previous tree state:
+	- JsonableTree representation of node/subtree

+```ts

+type NodeChangedEvent = {

+	changedProperties: Set<string>;

+	version: 1;
+	node: NodeHandle;
+	fields?: Record<FieldKey, DiffV1[]>;
+	truncated?: boolean;

body: I like this idea. It's simple. It's also intuitive. When you get the event, the change "already happened" - you're just now being notified of it. So subscribing to a node that changed as part of that change wouldn't re-notify you - because the change "already happened".

+	node: NodeHandle;

+
+```ts
+node.events.on(“nodeChanged”, newListener)
+```
+
+To simplify, we may consider the following:
+
+Minimum behavior:
+- Subscription changes (add/remove) that occur during the processing of a batch do not affect the current batch.

For example, if we emit an event on an arrayNode that underwent an insert operation, it may be useful to know which index the insert occurred at (or what was inserted).
+
+### Proposal
+
+---
+
+#### Option A: Previous Tree State Payload
+
+Provide the previous tree state in the event payload. As this feature is something that is requested by a few select customers, it may not be very widely used. If this is the case, simply providing a “node snapshot” of the previous tree state, and letting the users decide what to do would be the easiest way.
+
+We may consider the following to represent the previous tree state:
+	- JsonableTree representation of node/subtree
+	- shallow structural clone
+
+```ts
+type NodeSnapshot = unknown // exact type TBD
+
+type NodeChangedEvent = {
+	node: NodeHandle;
+	changedProperties: Set<string>;
+	getPreviousState?: () => NodeSnapshot | undefined
+}
+```
+
+---
+
+#### Non-goal: Exposing Raw deltas
+
+Although exposing the raw deltas may be just as easy to implement (while providing all the information we need), they are too difficult to read/interpret correctly for it to be a viable solution.
+
+---
+
+#### Option B: Normalized Diff Payload
+
+Expose an optional getDiff() function when subscribing to a nodeChanged event, exposing a read friendly, structured and versioned diff payload.
+
+##### Potential Solution
+
+Deltas stores edit information and maybe can be used to provide the diffs for the specific node we’re interested in. However, the information provided from a delta is not always isolated to the specific node, and the format of the deltas is difficult to read and understand.
+
+One way to implement this would be to create some sort of delta transformer function to isolate the relevant parts of the delta that we need and converts it to a read-friendly format. This data can then be sent through the payload when emitting the event. A stable versioned schema for diff payloads should also be considered to decouple it from our internal change formats.
+
+The existing trackDirtyNodes function can be leveraged to determine which nodes changed during the batch so that the delta transformer only computes the diffs for those nodes.
+
+We should also compute diffs once per transaction and expose them lazily or behind an `{includeDelta: true}` flag to avoid payload bloat. 
+
+```ts
+type DiffV1 = 
+	| {kind: “insert”; index: number; content: NodeHandle[]}
+	| {kind: “delete”; index: number; count: number}
+	| {kind: “move”; srcIndex: number; count: number; dstIndex: number}
+	| {kind: “set”; oldValue: unknown; newValue: unknown }
+interface ReadableDiffV1 {
+	version: 1;
+	node: NodeHandle;
+	fields?: Record<FieldKey, DiffV1[]>;
+	truncated?: boolean;
+	meta?: {origin: “local” | “remote”, revision: RevisionTag} // unsure if this is necessary
+}
+
+type NodeChangedEvent = {
+	node: NodeHandle;
+	changedProperties: Set<string>;
+	getDiff?: () => ReadableDiffV1 | undefined
+}
+```
+
+---
+
+##### Open Considerations: Event Subscription Timing
+Listeners may be added or removed while events are being processed, which may lead to strange behavior if we do not clearly define it. For example, we may have a weird case where we’re in the middle of processing a transaction/batch, and inside an event callback we do this.
+
+```ts
+node.events.on(“nodeChanged”, newListener)
+```
+
+To simplify, we may consider the following:
+
+Minimum behavior:
+- Subscription changes (add/remove) that occur during the processing of a batch do not affect the current batch.
+- New listeners registered from within an event callback will only see subsequent batched/transactions and will not receive diffs for edits that have already been applied.
+Additional guidance:
+- Document a warning that discourages registering listeners from within an event callback will only observe future batches.
+Stricter alternative:
+- Disallow registering listeners from within an event callback completely.

body: If bound to the "first n edits", then you miss out on the changes from the remaining n edits - that wouldn't provide a good experience, would it? Or are you imagining you could page through the edits? You could have it be a generator rather than an array.
