Looks fine to bump for now though.

## Pull request overview

This PR introduces a Domain Specific Language (DSL) for organizing and building prompts in the tree-agent package. The new DSL provides a structured, programmatic approach to constructing prompts instead of using string concatenation and template literals.

### Key Changes
- Added `promptBuilder.ts` with three builder classes: `PromptBuilder` for basic prompt construction, `ClassPromptBuilder` for documenting methods, and `InterfaceBuilder` for generating TypeScript interface definitions
- Refactored `prompt.ts` to use the new DSL, replacing string template literals with builder pattern calls
- Updated snapshot file to reflect the formatted output from the new DSL

### Reviewed changes

Copilot reviewed 3 out of 3 changed files in this pull request and generated 8 comments.

| File | Description |
| ---- | ----------- |
| packages/framework/tree-agent/src/promptBuilder.ts | New file introducing PromptBuilder, ClassPromptBuilder, and InterfaceBuilder classes for structured prompt construction |
| packages/framework/tree-agent/src/prompt.ts | Refactored to use the new DSL builders instead of template literals for prompt generation, particularly in `getPrompt`, `getTreeArrayNodeDocumentation`, and `getTreeMapNodeDocumentation` functions |
| packages/framework/tree-agent/src/test/__snapshots__/prompt.md | Updated snapshot reflecting the output format changes from the DSL, including reformatted JSDoc comments and method signatures |

The newly introduced PromptBuilder, ClassPromptBuilder, and InterfaceBuilder classes lack dedicated unit tests. While the snapshot test in prompt.spec.ts indirectly tests some of the functionality, there should be dedicated tests for these builder classes to ensure they correctly handle edge cases (empty parameters, missing optional fields, multi-line remarks, etc.).

The class is described as "A prompt builder for documenting class interfaces and their methods" but the naming is ambiguous - it's unclear if this is for documenting classes or interfaces. The generated output appears to be for standalone methods (not within a class or interface structure), which differs from InterfaceBuilder that generates complete interface declarations. Consider renaming to MethodDocBuilder or clarifying the documentation.
```suggestion
 * A prompt builder for generating documentation comments and signatures for standalone methods.
 *
 * This builder focuses on method-level JSDoc and TypeScript-style signatures, and does not
 * generate enclosing class or interface declarations.
```

The ClassPromptBuilder class is defined but never used anywhere in the codebase. If it's intended for future use, consider marking it with a TODO comment. Otherwise, it should be removed to reduce code maintenance burden. Only PromptBuilder and InterfaceBuilder are actually used in prompt.ts.

The `@remarks` tag is incorrectly placed before the `@param` tags when parameters exist. According to TSDoc conventions, `@param` tags should come before `@remarks`. The current implementation adds `@remarks` before parameters (line 127), which produces non-standard documentation format.

The ClassPromptBuilder generates two `@remarks` sections when both parameters exist and a remarks string is provided: one at line 127 (before params) and another at line 141. This creates duplicate `@remarks` tags in the generated documentation, which is invalid TSDoc.

This is cool, nice idea! I like how it is panning out below. Are you planning on also using it to handle the rest of the prompt generation above? The section below that has been updated is definitely nicer now, but it also wasn't particularly complicated to begin with. More of the "yucky" complicated logic is above. I'm curious to see how much clear it gets with this pattern - that's where the most benefit will be.
Seems like we don't need this method if we already have `addParagraphs`
Do you think this method, and the javascript one, and the json one, are necessary? We could just as easily supply the language string at the call site using `addCodeBlock`

Both this class and the interface one are pretty complicated. I don't know if they're actually helping to solve the problem here. In fact - ClassPromptBuilder isn't even used? Regardless, InterfaceBuilder is used, but it's making the array and map interface printing _more_ complicated, not less, IMO. Before, they were basically just big long string literals with a couple of small replacements - perfectly readable. Now, they are far less readable and they also have to be parsed by this complicated helper class before being printed. I think there may be some promise in the general builder approach employed elsewhere, but the interface builder is moving us into higher complexity and lower readability - we want the opposite.

I think we should just drop this. We _already_ expose fields on our nodes as simple properties to the LLM. It actually can't even tell the difference between fields and "additional" properties - they both just look like properties. I also expect that if it sees `readonly`, it will just respect it without any additional attention being called to it.

The reason we want to have special instructions for the methods is because we want to guide the LLM to prefer methods over properties.

You updated this file by essentially duplicating the "method"-related code and creating the "property"-version of it. Most of the code seems like it's essentially exactly the same. For example, this loop here is more or less identical to the loop above. There's code above where the same thing is happening - the code is pretty much identical, most of the logic doesn't do anything different for the method case vs. the property case.

Do you think things would fall out more simply if we processed methods and properties together? Instead of doing everything once for `getBoundMethods` and once again for `getBoundProperties`, we could have one pass that iterates over both. Or maybe `getBoundProperties` should just include methods, since methods are technically properties. For the small places where the difference matters, I think we can tell if we're dealing with a property vs a method by checking the type of the class holding the metadata.

I'm not sure how far it can go, but I do get the sense that a lot of the new code in here can be collapsed in this way.
What are these used for?

## Pull Request Overview

This PR promotes the `staged`, `types`, `stagedRecursive`, and `typesRecursive` APIs from alpha to beta stability level by moving them from `SchemaFactoryAlpha` to `SchemaFactoryBeta`, along with related type definitions and documentation updates.

Key changes:
- Move `SchemaStaticsBeta` interface and related APIs from `SchemaFactoryAlpha` to `SchemaFactoryBeta`
- Update all documentation references from `SchemaStaticsAlpha` to `SchemaStaticsBeta`
- Change API stability tags from `@alpha` to `@beta` for related types and interfaces

### Reviewed Changes

Copilot reviewed 20 out of 20 changed files in this pull request and generated 4 comments.

## Pull Request Overview

This PR adds support for using the `delete` operator on optional fields in ObjectNodes, making it functionally equivalent to assigning `undefined` to those fields.

**Key Changes:**
- Replaced error-throwing `deleteProperty` handler with implementation that calls `applyFieldChange` with `undefined`
- Refactored field assignment logic into a new `applyFieldChange` helper function
- Updated test from expecting an error to validating the delete behavior
- Added documentation explaining field assignment and deletion semantics

### Reviewed Changes

Copilot reviewed 3 out of 3 changed files in this pull request and generated 1 comment.

| File | Description |
| ---- | ----------- |
| packages/dds/tree/src/simple-tree/node-kinds/object/objectNode.ts | Implements delete support via new `applyFieldChange` helper and updated proxy `deleteProperty` handler |
| packages/dds/tree/src/test/simple-tree/node-kinds/object/objectNode.spec.ts | Updates test to validate delete behavior instead of expecting error |
| .changeset/fifty-crabs-add.md | Documents the new feature for changelog |

Why is the implementation here significant? At first thought, I would expect that `deleteProperty` would more or less be implemented as doing whatever `set` does, but hardcoded to pass `undefined`. Can we factor something out here?

When writing asserts (from @fluidframework/core-utils), please use a string literal for the error message, not a hex assert code. This instruction should only apply to newly added asserts, not existing ones.

pr_title: setting recordNode value to undefined behaves like delete
body:
## Pull Request Overview

This PR adds documentation and test coverage for the behavior where assigning `undefined` to a key in `TreeRecordNode` behaves like a `delete` operation, removing the field's contents. This aligns `TreeRecordNode` behavior with `TreeObjectNode`.

- Added test case to verify that setting a record field to `undefined` removes the entry
- Added documentation explaining the `undefined`-as-delete behavior with a code example

### Reviewed Changes

Copilot reviewed 2 out of 2 changed files in this pull request and generated 1 comment.

| File | Description |
| ---- | ----------- |
| packages/dds/tree/src/test/simple-tree/node-kinds/recordNode.spec.ts | Added test case verifying that assigning `undefined` to a field behaves as deletion |
| packages/dds/tree/src/simple-tree/node-kinds/record/recordNodeTypes.ts | Added documentation section explaining field assignment and deletion behavior, including an example |

body: It's still a good test to add, regardless of implementation changes.

pr_title: setting recordNode value to undefined behaves like delete
body: Does this new code path do anything different than the existing code path? In other words, did we not already treat undefined as delete? If we did, I think that adding this extra path is unnecessary, and maybe the only real task here (besides adding the new test, which is good) is to update the documentation on TreeRecordNode to make it clear that this is a way that we deviate from the typical JS behavior of "records".

Missing semicolon at the end of the statement. It should be `record.a = undefined;` to be consistent with the previous line.
```suggestion
 * record.a = undefined;
```

@@ -26,6 +26,22 @@ import type { RestrictiveStringRecord } from "../../../util/index.js";
  * Therefore code assigning to these fields must explicitly construct nodes using the schema's constructor or create method,
  * or using some other method like {@link (TreeAlpha:interface).create}.
  *
+ * Field Assignment and Deletion
+ *
+ * Unlike JavaScript's `Record` type, assigning `undefined` to a key in {@link TreeRecordNode} behaves like a `delete` operation,
+ * removing the field's contents.
+ *
+ * This is to stay consistent with {@link TreeObjectNode} behavior.
+ *
+ * Example:
+ * ```ts
+ * const Numbers = schemaFactory.record("Numbers", schemaFactory.number);
+ * const record = new Numbers({ a:1, b:2 });
+ *
+ * // This is equivalent to delete record.a, and removes the entry.
+ * record.a = undefined

pr_title: Add DetachedFieldIndex unit tests, and fix createEntry bug
body:
## Pull Request Overview

This PR adds comprehensive unit tests for the `DetachedFieldIndex` class and fixes a bug in the `createEntry` method where only one root ID was being mapped correctly when creating multiple entries.

- Adds extensive unit test coverage for all public methods of `DetachedFieldIndex`
- Fixes a bug in `createEntry` where `latestRelevantRevisionToFields` mapping was incorrect for count > 1
- Includes helper functions to streamline test setup and data creation

### Reviewed Changes

Copilot reviewed 2 out of 2 changed files in this pull request and generated no comments.

| File | Description |
| ---- | ----------- |
| packages/dds/tree/src/test/core/tree/detachedFieldIndex.spec.ts | Adds comprehensive unit tests covering all DetachedFieldIndex methods including edge cases and multi-entry scenarios |
| packages/dds/tree/src/core/tree/detachedFieldIndex.ts | Fixes bug in createEntry method where root ID indexing was incorrect for multiple entries |

pr_title: Add identifier API methods to TreeAlpha
body: "lengthen", for symmetry? What do you think?

pr_title: Add identifier API methods to TreeAlpha
body: If you can figure out how to word it well, consider putting a clause in this doc that talks about how it relates to `shorten` and `expand`. Likewise, put a note in the docs for `shorten` and the docs for `expand` that talk about how they are related to each other. For example, "Shortening and then lengthening an ID will return the same ID", or "getShort returns the [shortened](link to shorten function) string if possible" - that sort of thing. I think it will help users understand what's going on more quickly. Above all, the most important thing is to use the same terminology - always talk about "short" and "shortened" for short Ids and always talk about "expanded" for stable IDs - don't say "stable ID" since the user doesn't know what that is.

pr_title: Add identifier API methods to TreeAlpha
body: How about just `generate`? Callers will be writing the part before - `Tree.identifier.generateIdentifier(...)` - so `Tree.identifier.generate(...)` is sufficient.

pr_title: Add identifier API methods to TreeAlpha
body:
This function is a little complicated - do we need it anymore as-is? What if instead, we had `getShort` just return a `number | undefined`? Any user that wants the current (kind of weird) behavior can simply do:

`Tree.identifier.getShort(node) ?? Tree.identifier(node)`

Then the behavior is self evident, and it will be easier to document. It also gives users a way to more easily get a short ID when they are confident that the node has one (i.e. they know that they always let the system generate the ID), so there's no need to check that it isn't a string.

pr_title: Add identifier API methods to TreeAlpha
body: That's fine, makes sense!

pr_title: Add identifier API methods to TreeAlpha
body: Great thorough test suites!!

pr_title: Add identifier API methods to TreeAlpha
body:
This is fine, if you prefer it. If you would rather remove the boilerplate though, this would be an appropriate scenario to simply use a hardcoded string as the parameter to `getIdentifierFromNode`. e.g.

`getIdentifierFromNode(node: TreeNode, compression: "preferCompressed")`
`getIdentifierFromNode(node: TreeNode, compression: "compressed")`
`getIdentifierFromNode(node: TreeNode, compression: "uncompressed")`
`getIdentifierFromNode(node: TreeNode, compression: "preferCompressed" | "compressed" | "uncompressed")`

Given the name of the parameter and the string values, I think it's self documenting, so you could get rid of the whole exported enum and docs. Totally up to you though, nothing wrong with an enum if you like that better.

pr_title: Add identifier API methods to TreeAlpha
body:
I would suggest fleshing out this changeset even more. Think of it as a usage guide. If a customer were to ask you "How do I use the shorten and lengthen functions?", you can link them this file in github and it should be sufficient to explain everything. To that end, give some examples of (somewhat) realistic code that is making identifiers, shortening them, lengthening them, etc. and has comments talking about what's going on. Create a schema node that has an identifier and show an example of using an automatic system identifier, a user-provided identifier, and a pre-generated system identifier. Show them being shortened and lengthened.

Alternatively, if you think this collection of features is sufficiently complicated, then you could forego doing a detailed changeset here and instead write a usage guide and check it in separately. The changeset is nice though because it will automatically be put in our release notes.

body: What do you think about `TreeAlpha.identifier.create(branch: TreeBranch)`? It's shorter, and it might vibe a little better with the rest of our library - we have `TreeAlpha.create()`, for example. "Generate" might also imply that there's some computation being done, but one of the cool things about our IDs is that they are really cheap to make.

@@ -0,0 +1,96 @@
+---
+"fluid-framework": minor
+"@fluidframework/tree": minor
+"__section": feature
+---
+TreeAlpha identifier APIs for converting, retrieving, and generating identifiers.
+
+## TreeAlpha.identifier
+You can retrieve the long identifier with `TreeAlpha.identifier(node)`, where `node` is a `TreeNode`.
+If the identifier cannot be returned, it will return `undefined`.
+
+### Example for a valid node with identifier
+```typescript
+// This returns the long identifier from the node.
+const longIdentifier = TreeAlpha.identifier(node)
+```
+
+### Examples for when you get undefined
+In some cases, this method will be unable to retrieve an identifier.
+This will occur in the following cases:
+- The node does not contain an identifier field.
+- The node is an non-hydrated node with a user provided identifier. Note that if it is an non-hydrated node without an identifier provided, it will throw an error.
+
+```typescript
+// This returns undefined.
+const longIdentifier = TreeAlpha.identifier(node)
+```

pr_title: Dropdown to change column type in table example app
body:
## Pull Request Overview

This PR adds a dropdown to allow users to change the column type in the table example app.  
- Added a new handler (handleChangeColumnHint) in TableView to update a column’s hint property.  
- Updated TableHeaderView to accept and utilize the new onChangeColumnHint prop, and adjusted the layout for header cells.

### Reviewed Changes

Copilot reviewed 2 out of 2 changed files in this pull request and generated 1 comment.

| File | Description |
| ---- | ----------- |
| examples/data-objects/table-tree/src/Table/tableView.tsx | Introduced handleChangeColumnHint function and passed it as a prop to update column hints. |
| examples/data-objects/table-tree/src/Table/tableHeaderView.tsx | Updated the header view to include a dropdown for column type selection and cleaned up UI layout. |

body:
If I'm reading this right, it looks like we always allow the user to interact with the drop-down to change the type. But if the column has any cells, then we don't allow the update to go through.

It would probably be better to make it such that the UI disallows interacting with the drop-down when the column has any populated cells.

Note that in either case, due to collaboration, it will be possible for the type value to change while a column contains cells (e.g., one user makes the type change through the UI, and another user inserts a cell at the same time). So we'll also want some form of validation / handling of invalid cell content under a column. A "simple" way to do this might just be to highlight the cell in red if its content type doesn't match the column type.
- We don't need to add this handling in this PR, just noting that we'll need to tolerate cell data whose type disagrees with the column type (so we shouldn't throw an error or anything).

I would recommend moving these types into the same modules as the components that use them. I think that's pretty standard practice.

Nit: this comment is mostly expressed in terms of type information, which is already captured by the type-system itself. I generally recommend writing summary comments in terms of raw semantics as much as possible. E.g. "A node representing date and time information...". We probably want to link to documentation for JavaScript's `Date` type as well, since the underlying format we are persisting is based on that.

Also, to make the type a bit easier to use, you could add a static `fromDate` function that takes in a `Date` object and returns a new `DateTime` instance. That way users don't have reason about the `raw` field.

pr_title: add missing unit tests for tableSchema
body:
I asked Josh about this when I was reviewing this code, and the reason this was wrapped up in this function like this was to avoid running the code in createColumn, createRow and createTable during test enumeration.

Generally is nice to reduce what code runs during test enumeration so operations like running a single test are faster, but also since errors that happen during test enumeration are annoying to debug since they prevent all tests from running at all: consider a bug which causes a crash in createColumn: that would be real annoying to debug since you couldn't just see the tests for it fail, since no tests could run for the whole package.

There is a tradeoff though: generally its cleaner to put schema in static contexts, which run during test enumeration.

I think it's worth preserving the work Josh did to avoid running these schema creations during test enumeration.

Also making Cell, Column, Rot and Table schema at the top level in this file makes it annoying to test tables with different Cell, Row, Column or Table types (ex: if you wanted to test customized Rot subclasses with added methods or something like that)

body:
PR description says we are updating from 1.7.7 to 1.8.4, but before this change we had:

0.28.1, 1.7.9 (two copies)

Now we have:

0.28.1 (two copies), 1.7.9, 1.8.4 (two copies)


This doesn't match the PR description.

I suggest you:
1. Checkout latest main. Confirm `pnpm i --no-frozen-lockfile` on clean main doesn't make any changes. This ensures none of these changes are unrelated to your PR.
2. Merge latest main into this branch. (This ensure the above check actually applies to your PR)
3. Revert the changes to this lock file replacing it with the one from main. This ensure that experiments and such you did with earlier versions on this branch have no impact on the lock file.
4. Run `pnpm i --no-frozen-lockfile` on this branch so it should only do changes which correspond to the changes you have in the package.json files in this branch.

That will likely get you a smaller diff.

Then you can update the PR descript to seperatly cover both:
1. The changes to the package.json files for what versions of axios we support being used with our packages (You are dropping support for axios <= 1.8.4)
2. What versions of axios we install for development and testing

pr_title: Update todo app to use tree
body:
## Pull Request Overview

This pull request updates the todo app to use the new TreeDataObject class and the associated tree-based data model. Key changes include updating entry points and file extensions (e.g. switching from index.ts to index.tsx), replacing the old todo model with the new tree-based model, and refactoring related UI components to work with the new tree API.

### Reviewed Changes

Copilot reviewed 10 out of 11 changed files in this pull request and generated 2 comments.

<details>
<summary>Show a summary per file</summary>

| File | Description |
| ---- | ----------- |
| examples/data-objects/todo/webpack.config.cjs | Updated the entry file reference to use the new index.tsx file. |
| examples/data-objects/todo/src/index.tsx | Introduced a new application entry point using the tree-based todo model. |
| examples/data-objects/todo/src/index.ts | Removed the legacy todo model implementation. |
| examples/data-objects/todo/src/TodoItem/* | Adjusted TodoItem exports and view to work with tree-based properties. |
| examples/data-objects/todo/src/Todo/schema.ts | Added a new schema for the TreeDataObject and related classes. |
| examples/data-objects/todo/src/Todo/TodoTreeView.tsx | Refactored the view to use tree-react-API and tree events. |
| examples/data-objects/todo/src/Todo/TodoTree.tsx | Implemented the new TreeDataObject data model for the todo list. |
| examples/data-objects/todo/src/Todo/Todo.ts | Removed the legacy todo implementation. |
</details>


<details>
<summary>Files not reviewed (1)</summary>

* **examples/data-objects/todo/package.json**: Language not supported
</details>

Left a handful of comments, but overall this looks great!

A few naming suggestions:
- `TodoTree` => `TodoList`
    - We don't generally emphasize the "tree"-ness of schema types and generally just focus on the semantics.
- `TodoItemTree` => `TodoItem`
- `TodoTreeDataObject` => `TodoDataObject`
    - At the end of the day, consumers of this object don't really care what the underlying storage mechanism is - they just want a way to get their `TodoList` out.

body: That's because `asFuzzView` does not return anything. It asserts that something is true after it has executed (maybe a better name would be `convertToFuzzView` or `fuzzify`, lol) - and then TS can count on that assertion in its type checking thereafter. If you prefer the return-style function instead, that's fine. But, think about how your function will behave if somebody adds a new property to `FuzzView` in the future. If that happens, you want your function to stop compiling, so that they know they need to go fix it. If that is currently the case, great! If not, the types need to better express what's actually going on.

pr_title: Add optional dispose flag for forks after transaction
body:
Is this better?

```suggestion
		super({ ...options, jsonValidator: typeboxValidator });
```

You want this to use `typeboxValidator` no matter what, correct? Otherwise it's broken? Or, is there a legitimate use case for a subclass of this which allows a different validator?

And if so, you'd still want to supply `typeboxValidator` if the subclass _didn't_ supply anything. So that'd at least be:

`super({ ...options, jsonValidator: options.jsonValidator ?? typeboxValidator })`

In either case, you can change the default value for the `options` parameter to be `{}`, and you should follow the same pattern in the other constructor of your new subclass (because _it_ could be subclassed).

Then if other code wants to use it (ex: not at the root) its name doesn't suddenly become wrong, and code using it know why it makes basic chunks (because its polymorphic).

pr_title: Pass down id compressor for in-memory identifier encoding
body:
Having to pass multiple things through a bunch of functions like this can be kindof annoying, and when one (or more) of them are option, its really fragile, since if any of the code paths forget to pass the optional one down, the code still builds and mostly works.

A design pattern for cases like this that is more robust is to group all the things you need to pass to all these places into a single object, and pass that around.

Before you change, the ChunkPolicy was filling this roll.

THis suggests two approaches that might work well here:
1. Make a new object that holds both the chunk policy and the new data (either as two separate fields of an aggregator object, or as a single object with both sets of data like `{...chunkPolicy, idCompressor}`
2. extend ChunkPolicy so that it has all the needed data.

I think either of these approaches would be fine here, but maybe its useful to be able to have a distinct type for a policy that does not have a compressor (ex: for things like defaultChunkPolicy), some maybe the fiirst option, and creating a new type, maybe we call it  ChunkCompressor thats `{policy: ChumPolicy, idCompressor: IIdCompressor | undefined }`

I don't think you need a type more specific than JsonableTree so just doing 
`const initialTree: JsonableTree =` instead of satisfies would probably be fine (and simpler) here.

That said, `singleJsonCursor({identifierField: stableId})` will give you an equivalent cursor, and is much shorter. JsonableTree is great since it can do any possible tree, but if you tree is valid in the JSON domain, there is a simpler option :)

pr_title: add encoding of identifiers in UniformChunk
body:
I don't think this is the ideal place to apply this logic. I think it would make more sense to do it in uniformChunkFromCursor. Specifically it (and also `insertValues` which it calls) should take in an optional idCompressor, document that string values will be compressed when possible if its provided.

This approach would avoid a second pass through the data, as well as avoid constructing the uniform chunk having to modify or copy its data array.

This would also make it possible in the future when we have optimizations for fast access to compressed identifiers for uniformChunkFromCursor to not decompress then recompress identifiers.

Also more generally, uniformChunkFromCursor will eventually get optimizations (like the above one, but also others) to allow it to process already compressed data without having to traverse it again, so integrating the logic in there will be necessary once we try and get that working efficiently and make 0 passes through the data in that special case.

We should probably put this error inside of the `isMapTreeNode` check. That means that if a user tries to insert a tree that is already inserted _and_ is out of schema, they will get the "already inserted" error rather than the "out of schema" error. I think the former is more fundamental because it indicates the user is doing something categorically wrong (re-inserting trees is always illegal, everywhere) rather than merely wrong in this context (the schema doesn't match up here, but it probably does somewhere else).
What is the reason for this change?

`identifierFieldKeys` should be a `readonly FieldKey[]` (in addition to the field itself being readonly). You can do this by making `identifierFieldKeys` private and adding a public getter with the narrower type. Or, if you want to have a little bit more fun and remove some lines, you could add a `filterIterable` function alongside our existing `mapIterable` function in our top-level utilities. Then here you'd just do:

```ts
this.identifierFieldKeys = Array.from(filterIterable(objectNodeFields.entries(), ([k, f]) => f.kind.identifier === identifierFieldKindIdentifier), ([k]) => k);
```

and no getter needed.

body:
I think there are two issues here:

`#updateGenerationNumber` is undocumented, and its name is confusing. This is a function, but calling it does not update the generation number. Calling this unregisters generation number updates! Consider doing this in the constructor. If you really want to do it during property static initialization time, use a static initialization block https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Classes/Static_initialization_blocks , or save it to a property with a more clear name if you actually do need to unregister at some point (I don't think we do)

I think this might not handle unhydrated nodes correctly. Using Tree.on instead of the flex tree APis should fix that as I believe that workes correctly in this case. Ensure you have a test which creates a node thats unhydrated, then as a second step inserts it into the tree. Make sure that case still works correctly, even though the underlying flex tree dummy node gets replaced with a real one (which would break your events here)

You can have a for-loop that operates over two variables rather than one, if you prefer (and if the linter prefers - in the past it has complained about `while (true)` loops, though I'm not sure if that's still the case). IMO the for loop makes it more clear that the two variables are progressing together in lockstep.

```suggestion
		for (let i = 0, value = this.at(0); i < this.length && value !== undefined; i++, value = this.at(i)) {
```

Though, I am curious - under what circumstances is `value` undefined? If we only iterate up to `length`, and we know that length isn't going to fluctuate during the iteration, then aren't we guaranteed that `value` is always defined? Since we don't allow `undefined` in our arrays as legitimate elements.

body:
We should call this `tryLocalize` since it is the the "try-variant" of `localize`.

Also, the parameter `key` should be typed `string` rather than `StableNodeKey`. Here is the rationale. According to this interface, the _only_ way for a user to get a `LocalNodeKey` (initially) is to generate one with `generate()`. That `LocalNodeKey` is _guaranteed_ to succeed when passed to `stabilize()`, and the resulting `StableNodeKey` is guaranteed to succeed when passed to `localize()`. These functions are all guaranteed to succeed so long as the user "obeys the types" - i.e. _they don't cast_. We got into trouble and created a bug because we did a cast. So, let's continue to structure this interface with the same assumption - _no casting allowed_ - and let's find a way to remove the cast you are doing at the call site. With that in mind, a function `tryRecompress(key: StableNodeKey): LocalNodeKey | undefined` doesn't make sense, because a true `StableNodeKey` will never return `undefined`. But a function like `tryRecompress(key: string)` does make sense - it's saying "hey, I have some string and I'm not sure if it is a StableNodeKey or not, but if it is, can you get me the `LocalNodeKey` for it?"

Hopefully that makes sense.

body:
Typically it's best to write "non-try" functions in terms of "try" functions, rather than the other way around, because it puts all of the interesting logic in the "try" function and makes the "non-try" function trivial to write as a wrapper around the "try" function. The "try" function here should accept a `string`, and if the string is a valid mock UUID* it succeeds otherwise it returns undefined. The "non-try" function (`localizeNodeKey`) should just call this "try" function and fail if it returns undefined.

*A valid mock UUID is one like `a110ca7e-add1-4000-8000-XXXXXXXXXXXX`, where XXXXXXXXXXXX is less than `this.count`.
Why this change?
You can use `?? fail("Key is not compressible")` to do this whole method in one line - it's the pattern preferred by much of the ST codebase.
You can use `isStableId` to validate that `key` is a StableNodeKey, and if so you can assume that the parseInt will succeed - no need for the isNaN check.
Or better, I would consider making an `isStableNodeKey()` type guard (which just calls `isStableId` inside) for maximum type safety.

This function now returns the "most compressed" form of the ID, if there is one, so I think we should reword it a bit. Maybe something like:

Returns the {@link FieldKind.Identifier | identifier} of the given node in the most compressed form possible.
@remarks
If the node's identifier is a valid {@link StableNodeKey}, then this will return a unique process-local integer corresponding to that identifier.
If the node's identifier is any other string, then this will return that string.
If the node has no identifier (that is, it has no field of an {@link FieldKind.Identifier | identifier} field kind), then this returns `undefined`.

Remove this cast - cast to string instead. We _know_ that value is a string, so that's a fine cast. We do _not_ know that it is a `StableNodeKey`, so that's _not_ a fine cast!

You're also casting to string below - you can just cast once above: `const identifier = field.boxedAt(0) as string`.

`key as StableNodeKey`

no cast! cast bad! :D

Use `isStableId()`. IdCompressor only requires that the input is a `StableId`, it doesn't care about `StableNodeKey`.
Update the doc for the parameter and the return type here, since `key` is not necessarily a UUID anymore.

+

body:
I see two options for this function:
1. It is a free function (not an interface member) and all it does is call `isStableId()`. You want it to be a free function, not an interface member, because otherwise every implementation of the interface has to implement it the same way - it is needless duplication.
2. It is an interface member, but it gives a stronger assertion. It ensures that the given key is a `StableNodeKey` but _also_ that it _belongs_ to this `NodeKeyManager`. This is because there's a scenario that I forgot about when we last talked: you might have a `StableNodeKey` from another app, or another session (or maybe you even have two Fluid containers in a single app, each with a shared tree, and you are mixing the StableNodeKeys from each together). In that case, you want to check that the `StableNodeKey` passed here is one of "your" `StableNodeKeys`, otherwise you can'd do anything with it.

I think it probably makes sense to just do both. Have a free function `isStableNodeKey(string)` and a method on `NodeKeyManager` called `hasKey(key: StableNodeKey)`. The implementation of the latter is simply `compressor.tryRecompress(key) !== undefined`.

I would recommend adding one more test for an identifier which is a valid UUID, but not a compressible UUID (i.e. not a stable key created by the compressor - just some UUID that passes `isStableNodeKey`)

pr_title: Refactor FieldSchema constructor to be private
body: the motivation for of this change was to allow us to keep this flexible construction API which can make any possible FieldSchema (even ones we don't want people making) internal to the package, while users of the package public API have to use one of the less flexible options from SchemaFactory. Thus we want to avoid marking this public: instead it shouldn't even be package exported: we should only need access to it to implement SchemaFactory.

It's okay for now to have an import like this. We have a couple of other dependencies like this already, where the proxies assume some stuff about the flex tree.

Alternatively, you could just do the conversion in this layer. You can get context.nodeKeys off of the field. You should be able to do weaker casts too. Right now you have:

```ts
class LazyIdentifierField<TTypes extends FlexAllowedTypes>
	extends LazyValueFieldReadOnly<TTypes>
	implements FlexTreeRequiredField<TTypes>
```

but identifier flex fields should be hard-coded to number types. Something like this:

```ts
class LazyIdentifierField
	extends LazyValueFieldReadOnly<[LeafNodeSchema<string, ValueSchema.Number>]>
	implements FlexTreeRequiredField<[LeafNodeSchema<string, ValueSchema.Number>]>
```

Then when you do `field.content`, you will get a `number`, so you don't need to cast to `unknown`.

Instead, how about something that more clearly explains the usefulness to consumers:

Incrementing number that is bumped each time the `AnchorSet` is changed. This allows consumers to cache state associated with a particular generation number and later determine if that state may have been invalidated using a comparison with the current generation number.

For example, anchor slots can be used to cache the removal status of a node to memoize repeated walks up the tree.

The default is not stated here, nor is it explicit in the implementation where this is used.

I good pattern I like for this kind of things is to export const that has the defaults (ex: const defaultOptions: Required<SharedTreeOptions> = {......};`

Then when used in Shared tree you can do: `const fullOptions = {...defaultOptions, ...options};

I think that approach scales better to more options, and makes it easy to change what the defaults are in a single place.

put the doc comment for each status on the enum member itself. This will improve intelisense fo them, and will make them not show up as undocumented here.

This function should be easy to unit test, so add some tests for it to this files tests file.

This isn't the correct way to do this check. A path that has this key somewhere along the path (but not as the root) should not pass this check.

I'd suggest adding a regression test for this case, but if you do the suggested change to getDetachedSequenceContainingPath I mentioned above, its impossible to have (or test for) this bug.
thes comments (here and below) also should be updated
since this is a method on a tree (and it also clarified in the return type), I think `tree` in its name is redundant, so we can shorten this to "getStatus()".

We arn't consistent about it, and don't document it anywhere, but one convention I like from my previous team it to make performance related TODOs more searchable like this:

```suggestion
		// TODO:Performance: This is a slow initial implementation which traverses the entire path up to the root of the tree.
```

Also, I don't like code which contains TODO's being duplicated: better to have one call the other or have them both call a shared helper which contains the TODO to reduce TODOs in the code and make them easier to implement without missing any ussages.
Also factoring this policy out into its own function will make testing the optimized version against it easier (the simple version can be moved to a test utility and used to test against if needed)

I don't think this is the right approach to detecting deleted for a few reasons:
1. if the editable tree node is deleted, accessing its cursor is invalid and ideally would error.
2. This will return true for any root field (the special root or any other detached field)
3. If using the path information, the same data from the anchor instead of cursor will be faster.

For the deleted checking, I recommend adding a method to ProxyTarget, "isFreed" which returns true iff free has been called. TO avoid having to tract ans extra property for that, it can compute it by checking this.lazyCursor's status.

Also, given that your implementation aparently has a bug (incorrectly reports deleted for root fields, and doesn't report deleted correctly), I'm guessing it could use some more tests to ensure it is and remains correct in these aspects.

This is fine: you accurately describe why the assert should always pass (and I agree its a valid assumption). The fact that there isn't a good way to avoid this assert is a hint that maybe we could provide better APIs since it means we have assumptions that arn't captured in the types. For example if we had:

```typescript
export function locateFieldAnchor(locator: AnchorLocator, anchor: FieldAnchor): FieldUpPath<AnchorNode> { ... }
```

You could call locateFieldAnchor at the beginning, then only check for undefined parent in once place (in the returned FieldUpPath) instead of in both the if and assert.

If you write that function (after https://github.com/microsoft/FluidFramework/pull/16950 goes in), it will also make testing a little easier since slightly more logic is moved into a simpler to test place.

That said, this code is fine as is, so feel free to leave if you want.

You should never see any status other than alive since if you have an ProxyTarget that has not been freed: the ProxyTarget holds onto an anchor, which ensures the AnchorNode doesn't get disposed, and if the AnchorNode does become dangling, it will have triggered its "afterDelete" event, which frees the ProxyTarget.
Look at the call stack and see why its erroring. Its possible you will need to avoid accessing something on a deleated tree thats invalid and/or move where the check for deleted trees happens.
would be good to include a comment on or in this test for why it does not return "removed" yet.

In both the node and field tests, you include an extra child, but don't do anything with it.

You should probably extend the tests so that they ensure the contents of deleted/removed trees are also considered deleted/removed.

You also only test the inDocument case at the root. either extends (and rename) those tests to check non-root cases or add addationl tests for non-root fields and nodes.

While there isn't much special logic for non-root nodes (so maybe just renaming the node test to "node returns TreeStatus.InDocument" is enough for it, as "root" nodes arn't that special, root fields had different code handleing them then non-root fields, so I think they deserve a could extra lines in the test or a separate test.

It should be impossible for well-formed code to try and look up a disposed AnchorNode, since the only way to look up an AnchorNode is from an anchor, which is a ref count that keeps the AnchorNode from being disposed.

Also most usages (including editable tree) shouldn't see the dangling state either, since they should really free their anchor when they see a delete event since theres no real point to holding onto an anchor after that point.

fyi--random.integer is inclusive on both ends. so you should really have been using clients.length - 1 here.

but should be irrelevant given my other feedback

pr_title: add targeted fuzz test for out of order undo
body:
i think it would be clearer to construct an array like

`const undoOrderByClientIndex = [0, 0, 0, 1, 1, 1, 2, 2, 2]` and then call `finalState.random.shuffle` on it.

pr_title: add targeted fuzz test for out of order undo
body: `Array.from({ length: opsPerRun * clients.length }, (_, index) => Math.floor(index / opsPerRun))` should do I believe
For this test, and for all tests being added, please group the changes into transactions (so long as they're on the same branch). Sorry this was not explained in the work item description.

It would be best to ensure each node has a unique value. Otherwise debugging the test could get confusing (e.g., "which of the two D nodes am I looking at?") and it might hide some bugs.

```suggestion
				foo: ["A", "B", "C", "D"],
				bar: ["E"],
```
Same for the other tests.

It just occured to me that it would be good to also test that we get the same result when first moving the child, then moving the parent.
Probably simplest to duplicate the test (remember to give them different names though).

The fact that we're moving to a root field is not really important here.
```suggestion
		it("can move a node out from under its parent, and delete that parent from its containing sequence field", () => {
```
let's chat about this (and then next two tests)

You could put something in the fuzz test state which tracks which trees had operations performed on them, then use that accrued state at the end of the test to unwind everything (probably in the reverse of the order that it was applied if I'm interpreting the test case name correctly).

This will require you to understand nuances of the reducer you're using e.g. how transactions interact with `undo`, but that seems doable.

SharedTree is supposed to be exposing an undo stack size in the future ([work item](https://dev.azure.com/fluidframework/internal/_workitems/edit/3625)) so I'm reticent to invest too much in something more refined than what you have here.
seems fine to me, assuming it's part of the spec that undo no-ops when it reaches an empty stack.
don't you need to synchronize client state before all trees will have arrived back at their original value?

pr_title: refactor compose vs individual fuzz test
body: since `ISharedTree` extends `ISharedTreeView` and you don't appear to be doing any sort of narrowing in the implementation, it seems like all of these functions should maybe just take in `ISharedTreeView` rather than the union.

pr_title: refactor compose vs individual fuzz test
body:
This change doesn't look necessary, and is potentially harmful: you're evaluating `generatorFactory` to produce a single generator, which once exhausted (that will happen in a single fuzz test) will no longer produce interesting values.

Is the change here motivated by some type-level issue? if so, seems like you should be able to declare `generatorFactory`'s type explicitly to resolve it.

pr_title: refactor compose vs individual fuzz test
body: do you plan on using this elsewhere? I think it might be better inlined into the applicable test, or at least nearby (i.e. in the same file)
